text: Active site architecture and metal–metal interactions in bimetallic iron–metal–nitrogen–carbon (FeMNC) catalysts remain critical yet poorly understood factors in enhancing the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic media. In this study, we perform a comparative structural and electrochemical analysis of FeSnNC and FeCoNC catalysts against their monometallic FeNC and SnNC counterparts. Deconvolution of catalytic activity using CO cryo-chemisorption reveals that while the bimetallic systems possess a site density of M-Nx centers approximately half that of the parent materials, they exhibit 50–100% higher mass activity compared to FeNC. This enhancement is driven by a significant increase in turnover frequency (TOF). Multi-scale characterization via electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy confirms the coexistence of independent Fe-Nx and Sn-Nx or Co-Nx moieties, with no evidence of binuclear Fe–M–Nx configurations. Furthermore, 57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy indicates that the introduction of a secondary metal shifts the active site distribution in favor of the D1 spectral component (associated with a higher TOF) over the D2 component. Our findings demonstrate that secondary metal doping serves as a structural modifier that optimizes the intrinsic reactivity of iron-based sites, offering a strategic pathway for designing high-performance PGM-free electrocatalysts.
